next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

By the time the front loaders hit their last hard spin, the back room had gone wet and close with bleach steam. Mira had a crescent headache behind her left eye where Yaret Sanz's soft questions still sat. The wetbench lights made everybody look a little undersea. Tavi was elbow-deep in a stripped nutrient pump, swearing at a valve with the easy affection he reserved for machinery and people he did not want to admit he cared about.

Jo shouldered through the curtain from the laundromat with an avocado-green egg timer clipped to her apron, a thing so old its numbers had nearly worn off. She trusted it more than any phone in the building. Behind her came a woman in a navy Tideglass housekeeping polo, still wearing her key lanyard and the fake smile a hotel made you practice for hallways. The lanyard had a plastic whale on it, chipped at the tail. She kept flattening the hem of her shirt against her hip as if she were trying to smooth wrinkles out of herself.

"Last one," Jo said. "Unless the city sends me a bishop with his guts out, we're closed after her."

"You told them ten," the woman said automatically, and then winced because sitting had gone bad on her halfway through the sentence.

"I tell everybody ten. I tell rich people noon if I think it will get them out of my doorway faster." Jo gave Mira a look that meant urgent and poor and keep it moving. "This is Luz."

Luz Mendez lowered herself onto the stool by the drain with both hands braced on the metal edge. Up close she smelled of lemon polish, sweat, and the cedar perfume Tideglass pumped through its lo...
open_hooks_to_carry: Sharpen Celia and Yaret as different kinds of institutional intelligence.
Show Mira's lie-making under pressure without turning her suddenly slick.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hs behind on rent, in a city where everything came with a contract and most contracts were knives. But poor people made clearer promises than the rich did. They made them in duplicate keys and split fruit and who got the only private corner.

"It means this is your door," Mira said. "You leave, you come back. You don't wait in a hallway like cargo. You don't get left on a hold shelf because I'm scared to say a thing plainly."

Lin stared down at the key. When they spoke, their voice had gone very soft.

"No one's ever given me one of these."

Mira almost said, No one ever gave me one either. That was not true. She had had keys before. She just had never mistaken one for anything more than access, never for an argument about personhood hammered down into brass.

"Well," she said, because if she got more solemn than this she might ruin it, "don't lose it. I can't afford another nine credits this week."

Lin laughed through whatever had tightened in their face. It was not much of a laugh. It was enough.

They stood, crossed the room to the front door, and looked back for instruction. Mira followed. Gabe's blue tag swung lightly from the ring.

"Lift first," she said.

Lin set their shoulder against the swollen wood, raised it the fraction the lock required, and turned the key once, then twice. The bolt drove home with the same ugly scrape it had always made. The apartment was still cramped, still damp, still carrying too much debt and three people's weight badly. But when Lin looked back at her with the key still in the lock, Mira heard the sound for the first time as welcome.
state_deltas_required: An internal audit and a tissue-safety inquiry converge, putting Mira under scrutiny while the evidence she needs is still inside CommonFrame systems.
Mira becomes the subject of a formal investigation and steals the first hard proof she can carry.
